title: The cleanest room I've ever seen
summary: Describe the cleanest or most organised room you have ever been in and explain where it was.
topic: The cleanest room I've ever seen
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences (150–250 words) about the cleanest or most organised room you have ever seen. Describe where the room was and what it looked like. Use present and past simple. Include comparatives (cleaner than, more organised than). Explain why it made an impression on you.
hintStarters: The cleanest room I have ever seen was in…
Everything in the room was in its exact place, and there was not a single…
Compared to my own room, it looked…

hintVocab.meaningVi: sạch bóng, không một vết bẩn

sampleText: The cleanest room I have ever seen was in my uncle's house in Zurich, Switzerland, when I visited him five years ago. He had a home office on the second floor that looked more like a display room in a furniture shop than an actual working space. There was a large wooden desk in the centre with nothing on it except a single notebook and a pen. The bookshelves on the wall held books that were sorted by size and colour, and every spine was perfectly straight. The floor was bare wood and completely polished, with no dust anywhere. There was no clutter at all — no loose papers, no cables, no cups. The room smelled faintly of wood and something clean, like fresh air. I stood in the doorway for a moment because I was almost afraid to walk in. My uncle laughed and said he spent fifteen minutes each morning tidying the room before he started work. He believed that a clean space helped him think more clearly. I thought about my own bedroom back home, which was much messier and full of things I never used. That room in Zurich made me want to organise my own space differently, though I have to say I never quite managed it.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: more like a display room in a furniture shop
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: giống phòng trưng bày trong cửa hàng nội thất hơn

sampleAnnotations.phrase: nothing on it except a single notebook and a pen
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: không có gì trên đó ngoài một cuốn sổ và một cây bút
được sắp xếp theo kích thước và màu sắc
hoàn toàn không có đồ đạc bừa bộn

sampleAnnotations.phrase: spent fifteen minutes each morning tidying
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: dành mười lăm phút mỗi sáng để dọn dẹp
Tôi chưa bao giờ thực sự làm được điều đó

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Comparatives with 'much' for emphasis
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Thêm 'much' trước tính từ so sánh hơn để nhấn mạnh sự khác biệt lớn: 'much messier', 'much cleaner', 'much more organised'. Đây là cách tự nhiên để thể hiện sự tương phản mạnh.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: Add 'much' before a comparative adjective to emphasise a big difference: 'much messier', 'much cleaner', 'much more organised'. This is the natural way to show a strong contrast.

sampleTranslationVi: Căn phòng sạch nhất tôi từng thấy là trong nhà chú tôi ở Zurich, Thụy Sĩ, khi tôi thăm ông năm năm trước. Ông có một văn phòng tại nhà ở tầng hai trông giống phòng trưng bày trong cửa hàng nội thất hơn là không gian làm việc thực sự. Có một chiếc bàn gỗ lớn ở trung tâm không có gì trên đó ngoài một cuốn sổ và một cây bút. Các kệ sách trên tường chứa những cuốn sách được sắp xếp theo kích thước và màu sắc, và mỗi gáy sách đều hoàn toàn thẳng. Sàn nhà là gỗ trần và được đánh bóng hoàn toàn, không có bụi ở bất kỳ đâu. Hoàn toàn không có đồ đạc bừa bộn — không có giấy tờ rời, không có dây điện, không có cốc. Phòng thoảng mùi gỗ và thứ gì đó trong lành. Tôi đứng ở cửa một lúc vì gần như sợ bước vào. Chú tôi cười và nói ông dành mười lăm phút mỗi sáng để dọn dẹp phòng trước khi bắt đầu làm việc. Ông tin rằng không gian sạch sẽ giúp ông suy nghĩ rõ ràng hơn. Tôi nghĩ đến phòng ngủ của mình ở nhà, bừa bộn hơn nhiều và đầy những thứ tôi chưa bao giờ dùng. Căn phòng ở Zurich đó khiến tôi muốn sắp xếp không gian của mình theo cách khác, dù phải thừa nhận là tôi chưa bao giờ thực sự làm được điều đó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Where was the cleanest room the writer saw?
options: In the writer's uncle's house in Zurich
mcQuestions.explanation: The room was in the writer's uncle's house in Zurich, Switzerland.

options: A single notebook and a pen
mcQuestions.explanation: The text says there was nothing on the desk except a single notebook and a pen.
mcQuestions.hint: Find the sentence about what was on the desk.

mcQuestions.prompt: How were the books on the shelves arranged?
mcQuestions.explanation: The books were sorted by size and colour, with every spine perfectly straight.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the sentence about the bookshelves.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a comparative correctly?
options: My bedroom was much messier than his room.
My bedroom was more messier than his room.
My bedroom was most messy than his room.
My bedroom was much more messy of his room.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Messier' is the correct comparative form of 'messy'; 'much' adds emphasis.
mcQuestions.hint: One-syllable and two-syllable adjectives ending in -y form comparatives with '-er'.

options: A lot of untidy things in a space
A bad smell in a room
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Clutter' refers to a collection of things that are untidily spread around.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about the opposite of a tidy, organised room.

mcQuestions.prompt: How did the writer feel standing at the doorway?
mcQuestions.explanation: The writer says: 'I was almost afraid to walk in.'
mcQuestions.hint: Find the sentence about the writer standing in the doorway.

mcQuestions.prompt: How long did the uncle spend tidying each morning?
mcQuestions.explanation: The uncle spent fifteen minutes each morning tidying the room.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the sentence about the uncle's morning routine.

options: Completely clean with no marks or dirt
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Spotless' means perfectly clean — no spots or marks at all.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'no spots' — what does that mean about cleanliness?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct comparison structure: 'The office looked ___ a display room than a workspace.'
mcQuestions.explanation: 'More like ... than ...' is the correct structure to compare two descriptions.
mcQuestions.hint: To compare two nouns, use 'more like ... than ...'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What effect did the clean room have on the writer?
options: It made the writer want to organise their own space differently
It made the writer feel jealous of the uncle
It made the writer feel tired
It made the writer want to redecorate
mcQuestions.explanation: The writer says the room 'made me want to organise my own space differently.'
mcQuestions.hint: Find the sentence about what the writer wanted to do after seeing the room.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'bare' mean in 'The floor was bare wood'?
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Bare' means not covered or decorated — just plain wood with nothing on top.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Bare' means empty of any covering or decoration.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Do you think the way a room is organised says something about the person who lives in it? Does a clean, tidy space actually help people work or think better?
